intermediate.question: What is the time complexity to insert an element at the beginning of a singly linked list?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
intermediate.explanation: Inserting at the head of a singly linked list requires only creating a new node and updating the head pointer, which takes O(1) time.

intermediate.question: In a doubly linked list, each node contains:
options: Only data and a pointer to the next node
Data, a pointer to the next node, and a pointer to the previous node
Only data
Data and two pointers to the next node
intermediate.explanation: A doubly linked list node stores data along with two pointers: one to the next node and one to the previous node, allowing traversal in both directions.

intermediate.question: Which data structure is most appropriate for implementing an 'undo' operation in a text editor?
options: Queue
Array
Stack
Linked List
intermediate.explanation: A stack's LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) property makes it natural for undo operations: the most recent action is the first to be undone.

intermediate.question: A queue follows which principle?
options: LIFO (Last In, First Out)
FIFO (First In, First Out)
Random access
Priority-based access
intermediate.explanation: A queue is a FIFO structure where the first element added (enqueued) is the first to be removed (dequeued), like a real-world line.

intermediate.question: Which operation is NOT a standard stack operation?
options: push
pop
peek/top
enqueue
intermediate.explanation: Enqueue is a queue operation. Standard stack operations are push (add to top), pop (remove from top), and peek (view top without removing).

intermediate.question: In a binary tree, each node can have at most how many children?
options: Unlimited
intermediate.explanation: By definition, a binary tree is a tree where each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and the right child.

intermediate.question: In a Binary Search Tree (BST), the left child of any node contains:
options: A value greater than the parent
A value less than the parent
A value equal to the parent
A random value
intermediate.explanation: In a BST, for every node, all values in the left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in the right subtree are greater.

intermediate.question: The post-order traversal of a binary tree visits nodes in which sequence?
options: Root, Left, Right
Left, Root, Right
Left, Right, Root
Right, Left, Root
intermediate.explanation: Post-order traversal visits the left subtree, then the right subtree, and finally the root node. Pre-order is Root-Left-Right, and in-order is Left-Root-Right.

intermediate.question: A full binary tree with height h has how many nodes?
intermediate.explanation: A full (perfect) binary tree of height h has all internal nodes with two children and all leaves at the same level, giving 2^(h+1) - 1 total nodes.

intermediate.question: Linear search has a worst-case time complexity of:
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
intermediate.explanation: In the worst case, linear search checks every element in the array, resulting in O(n) comparisons.

intermediate.question: Binary search cannot be efficiently applied to which data structure?
options: Sorted array
Sorted linked list
Sorted static table
Balanced BST
intermediate.explanation: Binary search requires O(1) random access to the middle element. Linked lists do not support random access, so binary search on them takes O(n) for the middle element lookup.

intermediate.question: In an interpolation search, the probe position is calculated based on:
options: Always the middle element
The value of the key relative to the range of values
A random position
The last element
intermediate.explanation: Interpolation search estimates the position of the target value based on its relative position within the value range, working best on uniformly distributed sorted data.

intermediate.question: Which search technique is used for searching in a balanced BST?
options: Linear search
Binary search (comparing with root and going left or right)
Hashing
Breadth-first search
intermediate.explanation: Searching a BST follows binary search logic: compare the target with the current node and move left (if smaller) or right (if larger), achieving O(log n) in a balanced tree.

intermediate.question: Which sorting algorithm guarantees O(n log n) time in the worst case?
options: Quick Sort
Bubble Sort
Merge Sort
Insertion Sort
intermediate.explanation: Merge Sort always divides the array into two halves and merges them in linear time, giving O(n log n) in all cases. Quick Sort's worst case is O(n^2).

intermediate.question: Which sorting algorithm is the best choice for nearly sorted data?
options: Merge Sort
Heap Sort
Insertion Sort
Selection Sort
intermediate.explanation: Insertion Sort runs in O(n) on nearly sorted data because each element needs very few shifts to reach its correct position.

intermediate.question: Which sorting algorithm is NOT stable?
options: Merge Sort
Insertion Sort
Bubble Sort
Heap Sort
intermediate.explanation: A stable sort preserves the relative order of equal elements. Heap Sort and Quick Sort are not stable. Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, and Bubble Sort are stable.

intermediate.question: Merge Sort uses which algorithmic paradigm?
options: Greedy
Dynamic programming
Divide and conquer
Backtracking
intermediate.explanation: Merge Sort divides the array into halves, recursively sorts each half, and then merges the sorted halves. This is a classic divide and conquer approach.

intermediate.question: What is the space complexity of Merge Sort when sorting an array?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
intermediate.explanation: Merge Sort requires O(n) additional space for the temporary arrays used during the merge step. This makes it not an in-place sorting algorithm.

intermediate.question: What is the average time complexity of search, insert, and delete in a hash table?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
intermediate.explanation: With a good hash function and low load factor, hash tables provide O(1) average-case time for search, insert, and delete operations.

intermediate.question: What happens when two different keys produce the same hash value?
options: One key is discarded
A collision occurs
The hash table breaks
The key is rehashed automatically to a unique value
intermediate.explanation: A collision occurs when two distinct keys map to the same index. Collision resolution techniques like chaining or open addressing handle this.

intermediate.question: In separate chaining, collisions are resolved by:
options: Finding the next empty slot
Using a linked list at each bucket to store multiple elements
Doubling the table size
Discarding the newer element
intermediate.explanation: Separate chaining maintains a linked list (or other structure) at each bucket. All elements that hash to the same index are stored in that list.

intermediate.question: In linear probing, if a slot is occupied, what happens?
options: The element is discarded
The next consecutive slot is checked sequentially
A new hash table is created
The element is stored in a separate overflow area only
intermediate.explanation: Linear probing checks the next slot (index+1, index+2, ...) until an empty slot is found. This can cause clustering, which degrades performance.

intermediate.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of searching in a hash table with chaining?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
intermediate.explanation: In the worst case, all keys hash to the same bucket, creating a single linked list of n elements, making search O(n).

intermediate.question: An AVL tree maintains balance by ensuring that for every node, the height difference between left and right subtrees is at most:
intermediate.explanation: An AVL tree is a self-balancing BST where the balance factor (height difference of left and right subtrees) of every node is -1, 0, or 1.

intermediate.question: Which traversal of a BST gives elements in sorted (ascending) order?
options: Pre-order
Post-order
In-order
Level-order
intermediate.explanation: In-order traversal (Left-Root-Right) of a BST visits nodes in ascending order of their values.

intermediate.question: A heap is a complete binary tree where the value of each node is:
options: Random
Greater than or equal to (max-heap) or less than or equal to (min-heap) its children
Always equal to its children
Sorted left to right
intermediate.explanation: In a max-heap, every parent is >= its children. In a min-heap, every parent is <= its children. This is the heap property.

intermediate.question: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an unsorted array using the optimal bottom-up approach?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
intermediate.explanation: The bottom-up heap construction (heapify from the last non-leaf node upward) runs in O(n) time, which is more efficient than inserting elements one by one.

intermediate.question: Which data structure is used in Depth-First Search (DFS)?
options: Queue
Priority Queue
Stack (or recursion)
Hash Map
intermediate.explanation: DFS uses a stack (explicitly or via recursion's call stack) to explore as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking.

intermediate.question: What is the time complexity of BFS on a graph with V vertices and E edges (using adjacency list)?
options: O(V)
O(E)
O(V + E)
O(V * E)
intermediate.explanation: BFS visits every vertex once and examines every edge once, giving O(V + E) time complexity with an adjacency list representation.

intermediate.question: Dijkstra's algorithm is used to find:
options: Minimum spanning tree
Shortest path from a single source in a weighted graph with non-negative edges
All pairs shortest paths
Maximum flow in a network
intermediate.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices, but it requires all edge weights to be non-negative.

intermediate.question: Which algorithm computes the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) of a graph?
options: Dijkstra's algorithm

Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm

intermediate.question: Topological sorting is possible only on:
options: Any graph
Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
Undirected graphs
Complete graphs
intermediate.explanation: Topological sort produces a linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge u->v, u comes before v. This is only possible if the graph has no cycles.

intermediate.question: Which algorithmic paradigm does the 0/1 Knapsack problem typically use?
options: Greedy
Divide and Conquer
Dynamic Programming
Brute Force only
intermediate.explanation: The 0/1 Knapsack problem has overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure, making it a classic dynamic programming problem.

intermediate.question: The greedy approach works correctly for which of the following problems?
options: 0/1 Knapsack
Fractional Knapsack
Longest Common Subsequence
Matrix Chain Multiplication
intermediate.explanation: The Fractional Knapsack problem can be solved optimally by greedily selecting items with the highest value-to-weight ratio. The 0/1 version requires dynamic programming.

intermediate.question: What is the key characteristic of divide and conquer algorithms?
options: They solve problems by making locally optimal choices
They break a problem into smaller subproblems, solve them independently, and combine results
They store results of subproblems to avoid recomputation
They try all possible solutions
intermediate.explanation: Divide and conquer splits the problem into independent subproblems, solves each recursively, and combines their solutions. Merge Sort and Quick Sort follow this pattern.

intermediate.question: Dynamic programming differs from divide and conquer primarily because:
options: It uses iteration instead of recursion
Subproblems overlap and their solutions are stored to avoid redundant computation
It only works on graphs
It always runs in O(n) time
intermediate.explanation: In dynamic programming, subproblems recur many times (overlapping subproblems). Results are stored in a table (memoization or tabulation) so each subproblem is solved only once.

intermediate.question: A Trie (prefix tree) is most commonly used for:
options: Sorting numbers
Efficient string/prefix searching and autocomplete
Graph traversal
Matrix operations
intermediate.explanation: A Trie stores strings in a tree-like structure where each path from root to a node represents a prefix. It enables fast prefix lookups, making it popular for autocomplete and dictionary implementations.

intermediate.question: A priority queue is most efficiently implemented using:
options: A sorted array
An unsorted linked list
A binary heap
A stack
intermediate.explanation: A binary heap supports insert and extract-min/max in O(log n) time, making it the go-to implementation for priority queues.

intermediate.question: In a Red-Black tree, which property is NOT required?
options: Every node is either red or black
The root is always black
Every path from root to a null leaf has the same number of black nodes
Every node must have exactly two children
intermediate.explanation: Red-Black trees allow nodes to have 0, 1, or 2 children. The other three properties (node coloring, black root, and equal black-height) are required.

intermediate.question: A B-tree is primarily used in:
options: In-memory sorting
Database indexing and file systems for disk-based storage
Network routing
Image processing
intermediate.explanation: B-trees minimize disk I/O by having high branching factors, making them the standard data structure for database indexes and file systems.

intermediate.question: What is the time complexity of the naive (brute-force) string matching algorithm in the worst case?
options: O(n + m)
O(n * m)
O(n)

intermediate.question: The KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) string matching algorithm achieves what time complexity?
options: O(n * m)
O(n + m)
O(m log n)
intermediate.explanation: KMP preprocesses the pattern to build a failure function in O(m) time, then searches the text in O(n) time, for a total of O(n + m).

intermediate.question: The Rabin-Karp string matching algorithm uses which technique to compare strings?

Binary search
Dynamic programming tables

intermediate.question: Which data structure is specifically designed for efficient prefix matching in a dictionary of words?

intermediate.question: If f(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 7, what is f(n) in Big-O notation?
options: O(n)
intermediate.explanation: Big-O focuses on the dominant term as n grows large. The n^2 term dominates, so 3n^2 + 5n + 7 is O(n^2). Constants and lower-order terms are dropped.

intermediate.question: An algorithm with time complexity O(2^n) is classified as:
options: Polynomial
Logarithmic
Exponential
Linear
intermediate.explanation: O(2^n) grows exponentially with input size. Exponential algorithms become impractical for large inputs. Problems solvable only by exponential algorithms are considered intractable.

intermediate.question: Which of the following represents the correct order from fastest to slowest growth?

O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!)
O(n) < O(1) < O(n^2) < O(log n)
O(n^2) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(log n)
intermediate.explanation: The correct growth order from slowest to fastest is: constant < logarithmic < linear < linearithmic < quadratic < exponential < factorial.

intermediate.question: The recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) solves to:

O(n log n)
O(log n)
